question: Define a bounded set in a metric space. Provide a precise mathematical definition and explain it in your own words.
expectedPoints: A subset S of a metric space (X, d) is bounded if there exists a real number M > 0 and a point a in X such that d(a, x) ≤ M for all x in S.
The definition means that the entire set S is contained within a closed ball of finite radius M centered at some point a in the space.
An equivalent definition: The diameter of S, diam(S) = sup{d(x, y) : x, y ∈ S}, is finite.

question: Prove that the union of two bounded sets in a metric space is also bounded.
expectedPoints: Let A and B be bounded subsets of a metric space (X, d). Therefore, there exist points a, b in X and real numbers M_A, M_B > 0 such that A ⊆ B(a, M_A) and B ⊆ B(b, M_B).
Let R = d(a, b) + M_A + M_B. Consider any point x in A ∪ B.
If x ∈ A, then d(x, b) ≤ d(x, a) + d(a, b) ≤ M_A + d(a, b) ≤ R. If x ∈ B, then d(x, b) ≤ M_B ≤ R. Therefore, A ∪ B ⊆ B(b, R), proving it is bounded.

question: Discuss the relationship between compactness and boundedness in a metric space. Is every compact set bounded? Is every bounded set compact? Justify your answers with proofs and counterexamples.
expectedPoints: In any metric space, every compact set is bounded. Proof: A compact set K can be covered by the open balls {B(x, 1) : x ∈ K}. By compactness, a finite subcover exists. The union of these finitely many balls of radius 1 is bounded, so K is bounded.

The converse is false: a bounded set is not necessarily compact. A counterexample is the open interval (0, 1) with the standard metric. It is bounded but not compact, as the open cover {(1/n, 1) : n ∈ ℕ} has no finite subcover.

The Heine-Borel theorem specifies that in ℝⁿ with the Euclidean metric, a set is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded. This highlights that boundedness alone is insufficient for compactness outside of specific spaces.
